learning_objectives: Define scarcity, opportunity cost, and trade-offs
Apply the concept of marginal analysis to everyday decisions
readings: Principles of Economics Ch. 1-2 by Mankiw
The Economic Way of Thinking (supplementary article)

lecture_notes.introduction: Economics is the study of how individuals, firms, and societies allocate scarce resources. Every decision involves a trade-off. Understanding these trade-offs gives you a powerful framework for making better decisions in your personal and professional life.
key_concepts: Scarcity: unlimited wants, limited resources
Opportunity cost: the value of the next best alternative forgone
Marginal analysis: decisions are made at the margin, not in all-or-nothing terms
Microeconomics (individual and firm decisions) vs. macroeconomics (economy-wide phenomena)
Positive economics (what is) vs. normative economics (what should be)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating the true opportunity cost of attending college: tuition plus four years of forgone earnings

review.value: Not just the monetary cost but the value of what you give up. The opportunity cost of this class hour is whatever else you would have done with that time: studying, working, sleeping.
Should you study one more hour? Compare the marginal benefit (better grade) to the marginal cost (lost sleep, missed social time). Optimal decisions are where marginal benefit equals marginal cost.

learning_objectives: Graph supply and demand curves and identify equilibrium price and quantity
Predict how shifts in supply and demand affect market outcomes
Apply supply and demand analysis to digital goods and services
readings: Principles of Economics Ch. 4-5 by Mankiw
Why Digital Goods Break Traditional Economics (supplementary article)

lecture_notes.introduction: Supply and demand is the most fundamental model in economics. It explains why prices rise and fall, why some goods are expensive and others cheap, and how markets coordinate the actions of millions of independent decision-makers.
key_concepts: Demand: relationship between price and quantity consumers are willing to buy
Supply: relationship between price and quantity producers are willing to sell
Equilibrium: where supply meets demand, setting the market price
Shifts vs. movements along the curve
Digital goods challenge: near-zero marginal cost of production
lecture_notes.demonstration: Modeling the market for streaming music subscriptions: how Spotify's entry affected the supply curve and equilibrium price

review.value: A change in price causes movement along the curve. A change in anything else (income, preferences, technology, input costs) shifts the entire curve. Confusing these is the most common mistake in economics.

Traditional economics assumes positive marginal cost: each additional unit costs something to produce. Digital goods (software, music, e-books) have near-zero marginal cost, which disrupts traditional pricing models.

class_schedule.topic: The Economics of the Gig Economy and Labor Markets
learning_objectives: Analyze how digital platforms have restructured labor markets
Compare traditional employment with gig work using economic criteria
Evaluate the trade-offs between flexibility and security in the modern labor market
readings: The Gig Economy: Opportunities and Challenges (Brookings Institution report)

lecture_notes.introduction: The gig economy has transformed how work is organized. Platforms like Uber, DoorDash, and Upwork connect workers directly to customers, bypassing traditional employer-employee relationships. This creates new opportunities and new vulnerabilities.
key_concepts: Labor supply and demand: what determines wages
The gig economy: platform-mediated, on-demand, independent contract work
Transaction costs: how platforms reduce the cost of matching workers and clients
Worker classification: employee vs. independent contractor, and why it matters
Monopsony power: when platforms are the only buyer of labor in a market
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating the true hourly wage of a rideshare driver after accounting for vehicle depreciation, fuel, insurance, and self-employment tax

review.value: Before Uber, finding a ride required calling a dispatcher, waiting, and hoping for availability. Uber reduced these transaction costs to near zero with real-time matching. This is the economic logic behind most platform businesses.

Employees receive benefits, minimum wage protections, unemployment insurance, and workers' comp. Independent contractors receive none of these but get schedule flexibility. The classification determines which economic protections apply.

learning_objectives: Explain how network effects create natural monopolies in digital markets
Analyze two-sided markets and the challenge of pricing on platforms
Evaluate the economic arguments for and against regulating technology platforms
readings: Platform Revolution Ch. 3-5 by Parker, Van Alstyne & Choudary
The Antitrust Case Against Big Tech (The Economist)

lecture_notes.introduction: A handful of technology platforms now mediate enormous portions of the global economy. Understanding the economics of platforms explains how they grow, why they dominate, and what market power means for consumers, workers, and competitors.
key_concepts: Two-sided markets: platforms serving two distinct user groups (riders/drivers, buyers/sellers)
Cross-side network effects: more sellers attract more buyers, which attract more sellers
Winner-take-all dynamics: how network effects lead to market concentration
Platform pricing strategy: subsidize one side to attract the other (free for consumers, charge sellers)
Antitrust in the digital age: are platforms monopolies, and should they be regulated?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing Amazon's flywheel: how lower prices attract customers, attracting sellers, increasing selection, enabling lower prices

review.value: Credit card companies charge merchants (one side) but subsidize cardholders with rewards (other side) because merchants will pay to access customers. The platform captures value from the side that benefits most from access to the other side.

Network effects create a positive feedback loop. The largest platform attracts the most users, which makes it the most valuable, which attracts more users. This concentrates markets around one or two dominant platforms.

class_schedule.topic: Cryptocurrency, Digital Payments, and the Future of Money
learning_objectives: Explain the economic functions of money: medium of exchange, store of value, unit of account
Describe how blockchain technology works at a conceptual level
Evaluate the economic arguments for and against cryptocurrency adoption
readings: The Promise and Peril of Crypto (Harvard Business Review)

lecture_notes.introduction: Money is a technology, and like all technologies, it evolves. From cowrie shells to gold coins to paper bills to credit cards, each transition changed how economies function. Cryptocurrency represents the latest chapter in this evolution, but whether it solves more problems than it creates remains hotly debated.
key_concepts: Three functions of money: medium of exchange, store of value, unit of account
Blockchain: a decentralized, transparent, immutable digital ledger
Bitcoin: digital scarcity, proof of work, and the energy debate
Stablecoins and central bank digital currencies (CBDCs)
The unbanked population and the promise of financial inclusion
lecture_notes.demonstration: Tracing a Bitcoin transaction on a public blockchain explorer and discussing transparency vs. privacy
setup_requirements: Web browser for blockchain explorer access

review.value: A blockchain is a chain of blocks, each containing a batch of verified transactions. Every participant has a copy of the entire chain. Altering any block would require changing every subsequent block on every copy, making fraud computationally impractical.

Bitcoin functions as a medium of exchange (you can buy things) but is a poor store of value (price swings of 50%+ per year) and a poor unit of account (nothing is priced in Bitcoin because the value changes too rapidly). This limits its utility as money in the traditional economic sense.

class_schedule.topic: Inequality, Automation, and the Future of Work
learning_objectives: Measure economic inequality using the Gini coefficient and income distribution data
Analyze how automation and AI are affecting different segments of the labor market
Evaluate policy proposals for addressing technology-driven economic disruption
readings: The Second Machine Age Ch. 9-11 by Brynjolfsson & McAfee

lecture_notes.introduction: The digital economy has created enormous wealth, but that wealth has not been distributed evenly. As AI and automation transform the labor market, economists are debating how to ensure that the benefits of technological progress are shared broadly.
key_concepts: Measuring inequality: Gini coefficient, income quintiles, wealth distribution
Skill-biased technological change: technology complements high-skill workers and replaces routine tasks
Automation risk: which jobs are most vulnerable and which are most resilient
Policy proposals: universal basic income, education reform, wage subsidies, retraining programs
The productivity-pay gap: why wages have not kept up with productivity gains since the 1970s
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students analyze automation risk data for their intended career fields using the Oxford Martin School's automation probability estimates
setup_requirements: Automation risk database access (Our World in Data or similar)

review.value: Ranges from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality). The US Gini is approximately 0.39, among the highest of developed nations. Nordic countries are around 0.25-0.28. Higher Gini means more inequality.

Technology increases demand for high-skill analytical and creative workers (higher wages) while replacing routine manual and cognitive tasks (lower wages or job loss). The result is a 'hollowing out' of middle-skill jobs.

questions.question: Explain the concept of opportunity cost and calculate the full opportunity cost of a four-year college degree for a student who could earn $35,000 per year working full-time. Tuition is $10,000 per year. Why does this calculation matter for economic decision-making?
questions.answer: Opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative forgone when making a choice. For a four-year degree: Direct costs: $10,000 × 4 = $40,000 tuition. Foregone earnings: $35,000 × 4 = $140,000. Total opportunity cost: $40,000 + $140,000 = $180,000. This calculation matters because many students only consider tuition when evaluating the cost of college. The true economic cost is nearly five times higher when foregone earnings are included. An economically rational decision compares this $180,000 cost to the expected increase in lifetime earnings from having a degree. Studies show the average college graduate earns approximately $1 million more over a lifetime, making the degree a strong investment despite the high opportunity cost.

rubric.key: Correct calculation including both direct and foregone costs
Explains why the full calculation matters for decisions

questions.question: A new AI tool makes it much cheaper for businesses to create marketing content. Draw and describe what happens to the supply and demand for human graphic designers. What happens to their wages and employment?
questions.answer: The AI tool is a substitute for human graphic designers. This shifts the demand curve for human designers to the left (decrease in demand) because businesses can now get marketing content through AI at lower cost. The supply curve for designers does not immediately change (the same number of designers are available in the short run). With demand shifting left and supply unchanged, the new equilibrium has both a lower wage and lower employment for human graphic designers. In the longer run, some designers exit the profession (supply decreases), which partially offsets the wage decline but does not restore original employment levels. However, designers who learn to use AI tools effectively may see increased demand for their complementary skills, creating a new, higher-skilled equilibrium for human-AI collaboration.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies demand shift with reasoning
Correct prediction of wage and employment effects
Considers long-run adjustment or complementary effects

questions.question: Explain how cross-side network effects work in a two-sided market using a specific platform example. Why do these effects tend to create winner-take-all markets?
questions.answer: Cross-side network effects occur when the value of a platform for one group of users increases as the other group grows. Example: eBay. More buyers on eBay attract more sellers (because there is a larger customer base), and more sellers attract more buyers (because there is more selection and price competition). This creates a positive feedback loop where growth on one side drives growth on the other. This leads to winner-take-all markets because once one platform gets a head start, the feedback loop accelerates its growth relative to competitors. A new auction platform cannot offer buyers the selection eBay has, so it cannot attract buyers, so it cannot attract sellers. The switching cost is not just leaving one platform but leaving the entire network of users on the other side. This makes it nearly impossible for new entrants to compete once a platform reaches critical mass.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of cross-side network effects
Specific platform example with both sides identified
Explains winner-take-all mechanism with barriers to entry

questions.question: Does Bitcoin meet the three economic functions of money (medium of exchange, store of value, unit of account)? Evaluate each function with evidence.
questions.answer: Medium of exchange: Partially. Bitcoin can be used to buy goods from merchants who accept it, but acceptance is limited compared to government currencies. Transaction speed (10+ minutes for confirmation) and fees make it impractical for everyday purchases. Verdict: weak medium of exchange. Store of value: Poorly. Bitcoin's price has experienced volatility of 50-80% in single years. A good store of value maintains purchasing power over time. You cannot confidently save for retirement in an asset that might lose half its value in months. Verdict: poor store of value. Unit of account: No. No economy prices goods in Bitcoin because its value fluctuates too rapidly. Prices would need to change constantly. Even Bitcoin transactions are typically calculated by converting a dollar amount to BTC at the current exchange rate. Verdict: fails as a unit of account. Overall, Bitcoin currently functions more as a speculative investment asset than as money in the economic sense, though this could change if volatility decreases significantly.

rubric.key: Evaluates all three functions with evidence
Correct assessment of store of value weakness

major_assignments.title: Digital Economy Analysis: Industry Disruption Case Study
major_assignments.description: Select an industry that has been significantly disrupted by digital technology (e.g., retail, transportation, media, education, healthcare, finance). Conduct an economic analysis of the disruption using supply and demand, market structure, network effects, and labor market concepts from the course.
objectives: Apply microeconomic analysis (supply and demand, market structure) to a real industry
Analyze how digital technology changed the competitive landscape and market outcomes
Evaluate the economic impacts on consumers, workers, and existing firms
Propose evidence-based predictions about the industry's future direction
deliverables: Supply and demand diagram showing the market before and after disruption
Stakeholder impact analysis: consumers, workers, incumbents, and new entrants
Data table with at least 5 relevant economic indicators (prices, employment, market share)
Future predictions with economic reasoning and evidence
References: at least 6 sources including economic data sources

criteria: Supply and demand analysis correctly applied
Market structure analysis (competition, monopoly, oligopoly) identified
Network effects and platform economics analyzed where relevant
Economic reasoning is rigorous and avoids common fallacies
All major stakeholders identified and analyzed
Winners and losers from disruption clearly articulated
Labor market impacts documented with data
Consumer welfare effects evaluated
At least 5 relevant economic data points
Data from credible sources properly cited
Data supports the analysis and conclusions
Supply and demand diagram is accurate and clearly labeled
Predictions are specific and grounded in economic reasoning
Acknowledges uncertainty and alternative scenarios
Clear, professional writing
Proper formatting and citation throughout
Rigorous economic analysis applying multiple course concepts to a real industry
Data-driven stakeholder impact assessment with credible evidence
Clear supply and demand modeling showing market transformation
Thoughtful predictions grounded in economic theory and evidence

quizzes.description: Regular assessment of economic concepts and analytical skills

problem_sets.description: Weekly problem sets applying economic models to digital economy scenarios

industry_case_study.description: In-depth economic analysis of digital disruption in a specific industry

participation_and_discussion.description: Active engagement in class discussions, debates, and economic analysis exercises

final_exam.description: Comprehensive exam covering all economic concepts and their application to the digital economy

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. Economics builds cumulatively, and missing sessions makes later material harder to follow. More than 3 unexcused absences will reduce the participation grade by 5% per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Problem sets are due at the start of class. Late problem sets receive 50% credit for one day, zero after that. The case study has staged deadlines with 10% per day late penalty.
course_policies.collaboration: You may discuss problem sets with classmates but must write your own solutions. The case study is individual work with a unique industry focus.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Fabricating economic data or copying analyses is academic dishonesty. All data sources must be cited. AI tools may be used for research but not for writing your analysis.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to economic data sources (FRED, Our World in Data, Bureau of Labor Statistics). Spreadsheet software for data analysis. No specialized economic software required.
